---
title: "Instructor Notes"
description: "Teach the course with active learning, beginner safety, and calm debugging."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [course]
order: 30
colab_notebook: "notebooks/lessons/instructor-notes.ipynb"
---
{{< include _includes/colab-link.qmd >}}

<!-- fcpython-chapter-lab: start -->
## How to use this chapter hands-on

This chapter is about **teaching the course**. Do not read it like a reference manual.
Use it as a sequence of short labs. For each lesson, open the Colab notebook
from the button above, run Example 1, write a prediction, and then
change one small part of the code.

By the end of the chapter, you should have one small artifact: a notebook, a
script, a trace table, or a project note. The artifact should show one working
example, one controlled variation, and one error or surprising result that you
investigated calmly.

If time is short, study one lesson deeply instead of skimming all of them.
A deeply understood example is one you can run again, explain in plain
language, modify safely, and debug when it breaks.
<!-- fcpython-chapter-lab: end -->


## Why this matters

Beginner programming lessons fail when learners only watch someone type. This
course should be taught as active practice: short explanations, short examples,
frequent predictions, small challenges, and calm debugging.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
- How should an instructor pace beginner Python lessons?
- What should students do every few minutes?
- How can debugging become a normal habit instead of a crisis?
:::

## Teaching principles

::: {.callout-tip title="Key idea"}
### Teach by doing

Use this cycle repeatedly:

1. explain the purpose;
2. show a Example 1;
3. ask learners to predict the output;
4. run the code;
5. ask learners to explain it;
6. make one small change;
7. give a challenge with a hidden solution path.
:::

This active-learning rhythm is inspired by The Carpentries lesson style:
predictable episodes, setup checks, short challenges, and instructor-facing
notes. The goal is not to copy their lessons, but to apply the same teaching
wisdom to this Python course.

## Setup verification script

::: {.callout-important title="Setup check"}
```bash
python --version
python -m pip --version
python -c "print('Python works')"
python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"
```
:::

If a student is blocked by installation, move them to Colab temporarily so they
can keep learning while setup is fixed.

## Debugging routine to model aloud

1. What did I expect?
2. What happened instead?
3. What is the error type or wrong output?
4. Which line should I inspect first?
5. What value or type is suspicious?
6. What is the smallest change I can test?
7. Did the change teach me something?

## Responsible AI note

::: {.callout-caution title="Responsible AI"}
Students may use AI tools for explanations, but they should not outsource the
thinking. Ask AI to explain an error, suggest debugging questions, or review a
small function. Students should still run the code, test it, and explain it in
their own words. Do not paste private data or secrets into AI tools.
:::


## Checkpoint quiz

::: {.quiz-box}
Use this OJS quiz to confirm the purpose of this support page.
:::

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "instructor-notes",
  "title": "Instructor Notes checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Instructor Notes?",
      "options": [
        "Instructors should teach Python through prediction, small examples, guided practice, quizzes, and debugging routines.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Instructors should teach Python through prediction, small examples, guided practice, quizzes, and debugging routines."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A course support page is a guide rail that helps learners and teachers stay safe and oriented."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after Example 1 works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include _includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}
